text: The surgeon completes the left-sided retro-rectus dissection and begins a crossover dissection in the upper abdomen. The dissection extends beyond the defect margins to ensure broad mesh coverage, reaching the patient's right semilunar line. The surgeon incises the sheath near the peri-umbilical hernia, pulling down the hernia sac and peritoneum away from the pseudosac.

The surgeon encounters a second hernia defect approximately three to five centimeters below the umbilicus, related to a previous C-section. The omentum is visibly incarcerated within the hernia, and careful dissection is performed to separate it while maintaining clear visualization to prevent injury to surrounding structures. The neck of the defect is visible, along with the incision from the prior C-section.

The video shows the anterior sheath largely intact, while the posterior sheath remains open. The limited tissue available for a preperitoneal repair is noted, highlighting the benefits of a wide dissection for retromuscular repair to address the hernia defects effectively.

The surgeon extends the dissection downwards by approximately five centimeters to ensure adequate coverage of the multifocal defects and diastasis. A larger hernia is visible, which is addressed using a retromuscular approach. The process of closing and placating the midline begins, with the posterior sheath being incised and the fascia placated for reinforcement. Mesh coverage is applied, extending to the upper portions of the dissection.

The surgeon uses a number one permanent V-lock suture to placate the lower abdomen, achieving approximately ten centimeters of placation before reaching the peri-umbilical defect. The muscle bellies are re-approximated, and the posterior aspect remains open. The decision to close the anterior or posterior first is noted as a matter of personal preference, given the small size of the hernia.
